content: The numerical experiment on the high-dimensional Lasso regression problem (Samples=1000, Features=2000) confirms the theoretical prediction of the VOS framework. The VOS-Accelerated algorithm achieves a significantly lower objective function value compared to the standard Proximal Gradient Descent baseline. Specifically, the accelerated method reaches the strict convergence tolerance (1e-7) efficiently, while the baseline exhibits a much slower sub-linear convergence rate.
keywords: The final loss of the VOS-Accelerated method is strictly lower than the Baseline.
The accelerated method demonstrates a linear convergence rate (steeper slope) in the later stages.
The console logs confirm that the accelerated solver requires fewer iterations to reach the target error.

content: The convergence trajectory plot (Log-Scale) visually validates the stability analysis of the VOS framework. The Blue curve (Target: VOS Accelerated) shows a steep, monotonic descent, indicating that the introduction of the momentum term successfully accelerates the optimization process without causing divergent oscillations. In contrast, the Red dashed line (Baseline) descends slowly, verifying the superiority of the operator splitting approach.
keywords: The Y-axis represents the Objective Function Value in Log Scale.
The Blue curve (VOS/FISTA) is consistently below the Red curve (Baseline).
he gap between the two curves widens as the number of iterations increases.

content: The experiment further validates the robustness of the VOS framework on ill-conditioned problems. Despite the design matrix having a Condition Number of 10 (simulating a "narrow valley" landscape) and the presence of non-smooth L1 regularization, the accelerated algorithm maintains a stable descent trajectory. This proves that the "Variable Splitting" technique effectively dampens the oscillations that typically plague standard gradient methods in such challenging optimization landscapes.
keywords: The algorithm handles the non-smooth L1 regularization term correctly via the Proximal Operator.
The solver does not diverge or stagnate despite the ill-conditioned input matrix.
The Adaptive Restart mechanism (implemented in code) prevents momentum-induced overshooting.
